content:
Modern confidence intervals grew out of **frequentist inference** in the early 20th century, when statisticians sought repeatable procedures for quantifying uncertainty from samples without assigning probabilities to fixed unknown parameters.

**Student** (William Sealy Gosset, 1908) introduced the t distribution for small-sample mean inference when population variance is unknown—replacing z critical values that assumed known σ.

**Jerzy Neyman** (1930s) formalized the **confidence interval** as a procedure with a guaranteed long-run **coverage probability**, clarifying that the interval is random and the parameter is fixed—a distinction still confused with Bayesian credible intervals today.

**Ronald Fisher** developed related estimation and testing ideas in parallel; the duality between CIs and hypothesis tests became standard in applied work.

In applied ML and Gen AI, analytic CIs for proportions and means remain common in experimentation platforms, while **bootstrap** methods (popularized by Efron in the 1970s–80s) extend CI construction to complex metrics where closed-form standard errors do not exist.

timeline.label: Student's t (1908)
timeline.description: Small-sample mean inference when σ must be estimated from data—foundation of t-based confidence intervals.

timeline.label: Neyman's confidence intervals (1930s)
timeline.description: Defined CIs as procedures with stated long-run coverage, separating them from Bayesian probability statements about parameters.

timeline.label: Bootstrap CIs (1970s–80s)
timeline.description: Resampling methods approximate sampling distributions for arbitrary statistics, widely used for ML metrics today.
